randomNumber:
# Generate random floating-point numbers uniformly between 0 and 1
random_float <- runif(1) # Generate 1 number
print(paste("Random float (0-1):", random_float))

# Generate multiple floats
random_floats_vector <- runif(5) # Generate 5 numbers
print("Random floats vector:")
print(random_floats_vector)

# Generate random integers within a specific range [min, max] (inclusive)
min_val <- 1
max_val <- 100
# Use sample() to draw integers from the range without replacement (if size <= range)
# or with replacement if needed. Set size=1 for a single integer.
random_integer <- sample(min_val:max_val, 1)
print(paste("Random integer (", min_val, "-", max_val, "):", random_integer))

# Sample multiple integers with replacement
random_integers_vector <- sample(min_val:max_val, 5, replace = TRUE)
print("Random integers vector (with replacement):")
print(random_integers_vector)

# Sample from an existing vector
fruits <- c("apple", "banana", "cherry")
random_fruit <- sample(fruits, 1)
print(paste("Random fruit:", random_fruit))
reverseString:
# Base R string reversal (requires splitting, reversing vector, pasting back)

reverse_string_base <- function(s) {
  if (!is.character(s) || length(s) != 1) {
    stop("Input must be a single string.")
  }
  # Split string into individual characters
  split_chars <- strsplit(s, "")[[1]]
  # Reverse the character vector
  reversed_chars <- rev(split_chars)
  # Paste characters back together
  reversed_s <- paste(reversed_chars, collapse = "")
  return(reversed_s)
}

original_string <- "R Language"
reversed_str <- reverse_string_base(original_string)

print(paste("Original:", original_string))
print(paste("Reversed (Base R):", reversed_str))

# Example with multi-byte characters (Base R handles UTF-8 well)
original_unicode <- "你好 R"
reversed_unicode <- reverse_string_base(original_unicode)
print(paste("Original Unicode:", original_unicode))
print(paste("Reversed Unicode:", reversed_unicode))


# Note: For more robust/convenient string manipulation, packages like 'stringi'
# offer functions like stri_reverse().
# if (requireNamespace("stringi", quietly = TRUE)) {
#   library(stringi)
#   reversed_stringi <- stri_reverse(original_string)
#   print(paste("Reversed (stringi):", reversed_stringi))
# } else {
#   print("Package 'stringi' not installed for stri_reverse example.")
# }
factorial:
# Calculate factorial using the built-in R function

num <- 5

# Use the built-in factorial() function
# Input should be non-negative integer(s). Result is double.
if (is.numeric(num) && num >= 0 && floor(num) == num) {
  fact <- factorial(num)
  print(paste("Factorial of", num, "is:", fact))
} else {
  print("Factorial is defined only for non-negative integers.")
}

num <- 0
fact <- factorial(num)
print(paste("Factorial of", num, "is:", fact))

# factorial() handles vectors element-wise
nums_vector <- c(3, 4, 5)
print("Factorials for vector c(3, 4, 5):")
print(factorial(nums_vector))

# Example with potentially large result (stored as double)
# Result becomes Inf beyond approx 170!
num_large <- 20
fact_large <- factorial(num_large)
print(paste("Factorial of", num_large, "is:", format(fact_large, scientific = FALSE))) # format prevents scientific notation if possible

# Example invalid input
# factorial(-3) # Would cause an error
fileExists:
# Check if a file or directory exists

file_to_check <- "r_exists_test.R"
dir_to_check <- "MyRFolder"
non_existent <- "NoSuchThingHereR.xyz"

# Create dummy file/dir
tryCatch({
  file.create(file_to_check) # Create empty file
  dir.create(dir_to_check) # Create directory
  print(paste("Created dummy file '", file_to_check, "' and directory '", dir_to_check, "'", sep=""))
}, error = function(e){ print(paste("Error creating dummies:", e$message)) })


print("\n--- Checking Existence and Type ---")

# Use file.exists() - checks for files OR directories
print(paste("'", file_to_check, "' exists? ", file.exists(file_to_check), sep="")) # TRUE
print(paste("'", dir_to_check, "' exists? ", file.exists(dir_to_check), sep=""))  # TRUE
print(paste("'", non_existent, "' exists? ", file.exists(non_existent), sep="")) # FALSE

# Use dir.exists() - checks specifically for directories
print(paste("\n'", file_to_check, "' is directory? ", dir.exists(file_to_check), sep="")) # FALSE
print(paste("'", dir_to_check, "' is directory? ", dir.exists(dir_to_check), sep=""))  # TRUE

# To check specifically for a *file* (not directory)
is_regular_file <- function(path) {
  return(file.exists(path) && !dir.exists(path))
}
print(paste("\n'", file_to_check, "' is regular file? ", is_regular_file(file_to_check), sep="")) # TRUE
print(paste("'", dir_to_check, "' is regular file? ", is_regular_file(dir_to_check), sep=""))  # FALSE


# Clean up
if (file.exists(file_to_check)) { file.remove(file_to_check) }
if (dir.exists(dir_to_check)) { unlink(dir_to_check, recursive = TRUE) } # unlink removes directories
print("\nCleaned up dummy file and directory.")
dateTime:
# Get and format the current date and time

print("--- Current Date and Time ---")
# Sys.time() returns POSIXct object (seconds since epoch, with timezone)
current_time_posixct <- Sys.time()
print("Current POSIXct object:")
print(current_time_posixct)

# Sys.Date() returns Date object (just the date)
current_date <- Sys.Date()
print("Current Date object:")
print(current_date)

# Accessing system's concept of UTC
# Sys.setenv(TZ='UTC') # Don't usually do this globally
current_time_utc <- as.POSIXct(format(Sys.time(), tz="UTC"), tz="UTC")
# Or simpler if you just need formatted UTC string:
# format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tz="UTC")
print("Current Time (UTC representation):")
print(current_time_utc)


print("\n--- Common Formats (using format()) ---")
# See ?strftime for format codes
print(paste("Default format:", format(current_time_posixct)))
print(paste("ISO 8601 (space):", format(current_time_posixct, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")))
# For 'T' separator, may need paste: paste0(format(current_time_posixct, "%Y-%m-%d"), "T", format(current_time_posixct, "%H:%M:%S"))
print(paste("Short Date (%Y-%m-%d):", format(current_time_posixct, "%Y-%m-%d")))
print(paste("Short Date (locale):", format(current_time_posixct, "%x")))
print(paste("Long Date (locale):", format(current_time_posixct, "%A, %B %d, %Y"))) # %A, %B are locale-dependent
print(paste("Time (HH:MM:SS):", format(current_time_posixct, "%H:%M:%S")))
print(paste("Time (locale):", format(current_time_posixct, "%X")))

print("\n--- Components (using format() or accessor functions) ---")
print(paste("Year:", format(current_time_posixct, "%Y")))
print(paste("Month (num):", format(current_time_posixct, "%m")))
print(paste("Day:", format(current_time_posixct, "%d")))
print(paste("Hour (24hr):", format(current_time_posixct, "%H")))
print(paste("Minute:", format(current_time_posixct, "%M")))
print(paste("Second:", format(current_time_posixct, "%S")))
# Access components via intermediate POSIXlt structure
lt <- as.POSIXlt(current_time_posixct)
print(paste("DayOfWeek (numeric):", lt$wday)) # 0=Sun, 1=Mon,...
print(paste("DayOfYear (numeric):", lt$yday)) # 0-365

print("\n--- Unix Epoch Seconds ---")
# Convert POSIXct object directly to numeric for seconds since epoch (UTC)
epoch_seconds <- as.numeric(current_time_posixct)
print(paste("Seconds since epoch (numeric):", epoch_seconds))
# Note: fractional part represents milliseconds/microseconds
print(paste("Seconds since epoch (integer):", floor(epoch_seconds)))
maxInArray:
# Find the maximum value in an R vector (equivalent to an array in many contexts)

# Example numeric vector
numbers <- c(10, 5, 42, 17, 8, 99, 32, -5, 0)
print("Vector:")
print(numbers)

# Use the built-in max() function
# Handles numeric, character, logical vectors. Returns -Inf for empty numeric vector.
max_value <- max(numbers)
print(paste("Maximum value:", max_value))

# Find the index of the maximum value using which.max()
# Returns the index of the *first* maximum value if there are ties
max_index <- which.max(numbers)
print(paste("Maximum value found at index:", max_index)) # R uses 1-based indexing

print("\n--- Handling Different Types ---")
# Example character vector (lexicographical maximum)
chars <- c("apple", "banana", "zebra", "cherry")
print("Character vector:")
print(chars)
print(paste("Maximum value (lexicographical):", max(chars)))

# Example empty vector
empty_vector <- numeric(0) # Creates an empty numeric vector
print("\nEmpty vector:")
print(empty_vector)
max_empty <- max(empty_vector) # Returns -Inf with a warning
print(paste("Maximum value of empty numeric vector:", max_empty))

# Example vector with NA (Not Available) values
numbers_with_na <- c(10, 5, NA, 99, NA, 30)
print("\nVector with NA:")
print(numbers_with_na)
# By default, max returns NA if any element is NA
print(paste("Maximum value (default, with NA):", max(numbers_with_na)))
# Use na.rm = TRUE to ignore NA values
print(paste("Maximum value (na.rm = TRUE):", max(numbers_with_na, na.rm = TRUE)))
